1: The Book of Consolation begins (chs. 30-33) with the standard formula: haddābār ʾăšer hāyâ ʾel-yirmĕyāhû mēʾēt YHWH lēʾmōr. The shift from judgment oracles to restoration oracles marks a decisive turn. The MKT preserves the formula as the opening of a new section rather than continuing the letter-narrative of ch. 29.
2: kŏh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr kŏtāb-lĕkā ʾēt kol-haddĕbārîm ʾăšer dibbartî ʾēlêkā ʾel-sēper — "Write in a book all the words I have spoken to you"; the command to write (kĕtōb lĕkā ʾel-sēper) creates a document — the consolation oracles are not merely spoken but preserved. The Book of Consolation is thus a self-consciously created literary unit. The MKT renders "write in a book" rather than "write on a scroll" for readability, though sēper most naturally refers to a scroll in this period.
3: kî hinnēh yāmîm bāʾîm nĕʾum-YHWH wĕšabtî ʾet-šĕbût ʿammî Yiśrāʾēl ûYĕhûdâ — "For the days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will restore the fortunes of my people Israel and Judah"; šabtî ʾet-šĕbût — the double-šûb restoration formula. Both northern Israel (Yiśrāʾēl) and southern Judah (Yĕhûdâ) are included in the restoration — the divided kingdom reunified. The phrase wĕhăšibōtîm ʾel-hāʾārets ʾăšer-nātattî laʾăbôtām wîrĕšûhā — "I will bring them back to the land I gave their ancestors, and they will take possession of it."
4: The structural marker introducing the poetry that follows: wĕʾēlleh haddĕbārîm ʾăšer dibbēr YHWH ʾel-Yiśrāʾēl wĕʾel-Yĕhûdâ — "These are the words the LORD spoke concerning Israel and Judah." The transition from prose (vv. 1-3) to poetry (vv. 5-7) is introduced formally. The audience is both kingdoms together — the eschatological vision overrides the historical division.
5: qôl ḥarādâ šāmāʿnû paḥad wĕʾên šālôm — "We have heard a cry of terror, of dread — not of peace"; the Book of Consolation begins paradoxically with a terror oracle. Before the restoration comes "Jacob's trouble" (v.7). The word ḥarādâ (terror, trembling) and paḥad (dread, fear) establish the severity of what must be endured before the restoration. Wĕʾên šālôm — "and there is no peace" — the peace Hananiah falsely promised is genuinely absent in the time of trouble.
6: šaʾălû-nāʾ ûrĕʾû ʾim-yōlēd zākār — "Ask yourselves and look: can a man give birth?" The rhetorical impossibility: a man cannot give birth; yet every man is clutching his sides kĕyôlēdâ (like a woman in labor) and faces have turned pale (nehĕpĕkû kol-pānîm lĕyērāqôn). The unprecedented nature of the coming distress is conveyed by an image of gender reversal — men experiencing birth pangs. The MKT preserves the rhetorical question to maintain the shock of the image.
7: ʾôy kî gādôl hayyôm hahûʾ mēʾên kāmōhû — "How terrible that day will be! There has never been another like it"; ʾôy (woe/alas) opens the verse. The phrase wĕʾēt-ṣārâ hîʾ lĕYaʿăqōb — "It is a time of anguish for Jacob" — the phrase "time of Jacob's trouble" used in subsequent eschatological interpretation. The climactic assurance: ûmimmennâ yiwwāšēaʿ — "but he will be saved from it." The salvation comes through the trouble, not around it. The MKT renders "anguish" for ṣārâ (distress, trouble, straits).
8: wĕhāyâ bayyôm hahûʾ nĕʾum YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ʾešbōr ʿullô mēʿal ṣawwāʾrekā — "On that day, declares the LORD of hosts, I will break the yoke from your neck"; the same vocabulary as Hananiah's false promise (28:2) — but now YHWH genuinely promises it for the eschatological day. Hananiah's premature "I have broken the yoke" will be truly fulfilled in the future YHWH envisions. The MKT's translation preserves the verbal echo of ch. 28.
9: wĕʿābĕdû ʾēt YHWH ʾĕlōhêhem wĕʾēt Dāwîd malkām ʾăšer ʾāqîm lāhem — "Instead they will serve the LORD their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them"; the Davidic restoration: Dāwîd malkām ʾăšer ʾāqîm lāhem — David their king whom I will raise up. Since David has been dead for centuries, this must refer to a Davidic heir whom YHWH will "raise up" (qûm in the hiphil). The phrase ʾăšer ʾāqîm lāhem (whom I will raise up for them) makes the Davidic king a divine gift given in the future, not a continuation of the present dynasty. The MKT preserves the future-oriented language of divine raising-up.
10: wĕʾattâ ʾal-tîrāʾ ʿabdî Yaʿăqōb nĕʾum YHWH — "So do not be afraid, my servant Jacob, declares the LORD"; the divine reassurance formula "Do not fear" (ʾal-tîrāʾ) addressed to "my servant Jacob" (ʿabdî Yaʿăqōb) — the servant-language for the nation. "For I am going to save you from far away, your descendants from the land of their exile" — the restoration is a rescue from mērāḥôq (far away), from all the lands of exile. The parallel "Jacob will return to safety" (wĕšāb Yaʿăqōb wĕšāqaṭ) — šāqaṭ means to be quiet/at rest, the opposite of the terror described in vv. 5-7.
11: kî ʾittĕkā ʾānî nĕʾum YHWH lĕhôšîʿekā — "For I am with you, declares the LORD, to save you"; the 'immanuel'-formula ("I am with you") as the ground of salvation. Three assurances: YHWH with Jacob, full destruction of the nations where scattered, discipline of Jacob in just measure. Wĕyissar tĕkā lammišpāṭ — "I will discipline you in just measure" (lammišpāṭ = according to justice). The guarantee: wĕnaqkeh lōʾ ʾănaqkek — "I will by no means leave you unpunished" — the discipline is real but bounded, not annihilation.
12: kōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾānûš lĕšibrĕkā naḥlâ makkātēk — "For thus says the LORD: Your injury is incurable, your wound is severe"; ʾānûš (incurable, desperately sick) — the same word used of the heart in 17:9. The wound (makkâ) is described with the word for a blow struck in battle: YHWH has struck Israel as an enemy would strike. The shift from restoration promise (vv. 8-11) to wound-description (vv. 12-15) creates a dialectical movement — the wound must be acknowledged before the healing can be received.
13: ʾên-dān dînēk lĕmĕzôr rĕpuʾôt tĕʿālâ ʾên lāk — "There is no one to plead your case; you have no medicines for your wound — no recovery for you"; dān dînēk (pleading your case) — no advocate; mĕzôr (medicines for a wound, a compress) — no physician. The medical vocabulary frames the wound as both legally and physically unaddressable by human means. The hopelessness established here makes YHWH's healing promise in v.17 all the more radical.
14: kol-mĕʾahăbayik šĕkēḥûk — "All your allies have abandoned you; they no longer care about you"; mĕʾahăbayik (your lovers/allies) — the political allies who were to provide security have forgotten Israel. The wound described: YHWH struck Israel makkâ ʾôyēb — "the blow of an enemy" — and mûsar ʾakzārî — "the punishment of a cruel one." The terrible irony: the enemy-blow comes from YHWH himself, not from the nations alone.
15: mah-tizʿaq ʿal-šibrĕk ʾānûš maʿkōbēk — "Why do you cry out over your wound? Your pain is incurable"; the diagnostic: the crying is understandable, but the cause is the people's own sin: ʿal rōb ʿăwōnēk ʿāṣĕmû ḥaṭṭōʾtayik — "because your guilt is great and your sins are many, I have done these things to you." The covenant-justice explanation for the incurable wound: the severity of the wound is proportional to the severity of the sin.
16: lākēn kol-ʾōkelayik yēʾākēlû — "Therefore all who devour you will be devoured"; the reversal oracle begins with a lākēn (therefore) that pivots from diagnosis to restoration. The three reversals: devouring devourers; captive captors; plundering plunderers — each attacker is subjected to the same fate they inflicted. The MKT preserves the wordplay in each pair (devour/devoured, captive/captivity, plunder/plundered).
17: kî ʾaʿăleh ʾărûkâ lāk ûmimmakkôtayik ʾerpāʾēk nĕʾum YHWH — "For I will restore your health and heal your wounds, declares the LORD"; ʾārûkâ (new skin, healing, health) — the medical term for recovery from a wound. The reason for healing: kî niddāḥâ qārĕʾû lāk — "because they called you Outcast (niddāḥâ), 'Zion for whom no one cares'" — the social shame of abandonment becomes YHWH's motivation for intervention. YHWH heals Zion precisely because the nations have written her off. The MKT renders niddāḥâ as "Outcast."
18: hinnî-šāb šĕbût ʾohŏlê Yaʿăqōb — "Thus says the LORD: I am going to restore the fortunes of Jacob's tents"; šĕbût is here used again — the restoration formula (cf. v.3). The specific images: the city (ʿîr) will be rebuilt on its mound (tel); the palace (ʾarmôn) will stand in its rightful place. Tūrăm al-mišpāṭām — "on its own site, in its rightful place" — the restoration is to the same location, not relocation to somewhere else.
19: wĕyāṣāʾ mēhem tôdâ wĕqôl mĕśaḥăqîm — "From them will come songs of thanksgiving and the sound of rejoicing"; the acoustic image of restoration: tôdâ (thanksgiving, praise) and qôl mĕśaḥăqîm (sound of those who make merry). "I will multiply them so that they will not be few; I will honor them so that they will not be small" — the reversed direction: from decrease to increase, from humiliation to honor. The MKT renders both pairs to preserve the doublet structure.
20: wĕhāyû bānāyw kĕqedem — "Their children will be as they were in former times" (kĕqedem, as in the former days); the restoration of the ancestral community pattern. Wĕʿēdātô lĕpānay tikkôn — "their congregation will be established before me" — the covenantal assembly restored. "And I will punish all who oppress them" — the covenant protection against oppressors is reimposed.
21: wĕhāyâ ʾaddîrô mimmennû ûmōšĕlô miqirbô yēṣēʾ — "Their leader will come from among them, and their governor will emerge from their own midst"; ʾaddîr (mighty one, noble leader) and mōšēl (ruler, governor) — both from among the people, not from a foreign power. The phrase wĕhiqqarbtîw wĕniggaš ʾēlay — "I will bring him near, and he will approach me" — priestly language: niggaš ʾēlay means drawing near to YHWH in the way a priest draws near. The leader who is both ruler and priestly-access figure anticipates the Christological theme of Melchizedek-priest-king. The MKT renders "approach me" to preserve the theologically loaded nāgaš (draw near) vocabulary.
22: wîhîtem lî lĕʿām wĕʾānōkî ʾehyeh lākem lēʾlōhîm — "You will be my people, and I will be your God"; the covenant formula in its shortest and most compressed form. The bilateral structure: your people (lĕʿām) / your God (lēʾlōhîm). This formula appears at the heart of the covenant (Exod 6:7; Lev 26:12; Deut 26:17-18) and marks its climactic restoration in both Jeremiah (31:33) and Ezekiel (36:28). Its placement here, mid-consolation, anchors the entire restoration to the covenant relationship.
23: hinnēh saʿărat YHWH ḥēmâ yāṣĕʾāh saʿar mitgôrēr — "See, the storm of the LORD! His wrath has gone forth, a sweeping whirlwind"; an intrusion of the judgment oracle before the final climax. Saʿar (storm, whirlwind) and mitgôrēr (sweeping, swirling) — the storm imagery for YHWH's judgment (cf. 23:19). "It will break upon the head of the wicked" (ʿal rōʾš rĕšāʿîm yāḥûl). The storm oracle is not against Israel but against the nations and the wicked — the same wrath that judged Israel will fall on oppressors. The MKT renders the storm vocabulary consistently with its appearance in ch. 23.
24: lōʾ yāšûb ḥărôn ʾap-YHWH ʿad-ʿăśōtô wĕʿad-hăqîmô mĕzimmôt libbô — "The fierce anger of the LORD will not turn back until he has done and performed the purposes of his heart"; mĕzimmôt libbô — the purposes/devices of YHWH's heart — what YHWH plans will be executed. The closing phrase: bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm tĕtbônănû bāh — "In the latter days you will understand this" — the consolation and the storm alike will become clear at the end. The MKT renders ʾaḥărît hayyāmîm as "the latter days" (the standard eschatological phrase) and tĕtbônănû (you will understand/perceive it) to preserve the future comprehension promised.
